description: Implement the LRS project per IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md. Each task (T1-T30) should be one PR-sized chunk. Follow the dependency graph exactly.
title: Implement the LRS project per IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md. Each task (T1-T30) should be one PR-sized chunk. Follow the dependency graph exactly.

description: Create the project skeleton and shared type definitions. DELIVERABLES: 1. pyproject.toml with deps: torch, transformers, reservoirpy, scipy, numpy, wandb, peft, datasets, ruff, mypy, pytest 2. src/ package layout: src/__init__.py, src/reservoir/, src/models/, src/training/, src/eval/, src/eval/benchmarks/, src/data/, configs/, tests/, scripts/ 3. src/types.py with shared protocols/dataclasses: ReservoirConfig dataclass, Reservoir protocol (step/reset), ModelWrapper protocol (forward/generate/get_hidden), BenchmarkExample dataclass (input/target/metadata), EvalResult dataclass (task/metric/value/config), Generator protocol, DataPipeline protocol (iterate/split) 4. Pre-commit config for ruff + mypy 5. Basic CI skeleton (GitHub Actions or Makefile with lint+test targets) ACCEPTANCE: pip install -e . works, pytest runs (even with 0 tests), linter passes, all protocols importable from src.types. REPO STRUCTURE: See IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md §7 for target layout. NO DEPENDENCIES - this is the first task.
title: T1: Project scaffolding & shared interfaces

description: Evaluate LLaMA-3.2-1B on full benchmark suite. Architecture control for the DeltaNet synergy hypothesis — LLaMA uses pure softmax attention (no DeltaNet). DELIVERABLES: 1. Load meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B via T3 model loader 2. Run through eval harness on ALL benchmarks (T5 synthetic suite) 3. Output: results JSON in results/baselines/llama32_1b.json 4. Script: scripts/run_baseline_llama.py or configs/baselines/llama32.yaml DEPENDS ON: T3 (model loading), T4 (eval harness), T5 (benchmarks) COMPUTE: GPU (~3h) ACCEPTANCE: Full results JSON.
title: T10: LLaMA-3.2-1B pure-attention baseline

description: Implement compressive memory baseline (Infini-attention or Titans/ATLAS style) on Qwen3.5-0.8B. This is Control 3 — the strongest non-reservoir memory baseline. DELIVERABLES: 1. Implement Infini-attention or Titans-style test-time memory module that integrates with Qwen3.5-0.8B    - Compressive memory that summarizes past KV cache into fixed-size memory    - Can reference Infini-attention paper (Munkhdalai et al. 2024) or Titans (Khodadadi et al. 2025) 2. Train adapter + memory module using LoRA training pipeline (T13) on same fine-tuning data as Track A 3. Evaluate on full benchmark suite, especially memory benchmarks 4. Output: results JSON in results/baselines/infini_attention.json 5. Implementation at src/models/infini_attention.py or src/models/titans.py DEPENDS ON: T3 (model loading), T4 (eval harness), T5 (benchmarks) COMPUTE: GPU (~15h for implementation + training + eval) ACCEPTANCE: Trained model, eval results on memory benchmarks, comparison vs T7.
title: T11: Infini-attention / Titans baseline

description: Implement the reservoir-LLM interface modules at src/reservoir/interface.py. These are the core modules that connect the ESN reservoir to the transformer. DELIVERABLES: 1. ReadProjection: Linear projection from reservoir state dim -> LLM hidden dim. Maps reservoir states into the LLM's representation space. 2. WriteHead: Linear projection from LLM hidden dim -> reservoir input dim. Extracts write signals from LLM hidden states to feed back into the reservoir. 3. CrossAttentionSidecar:    - Reservoir states serve as Key/Value, LLM hidden states serve as Query    - Insertable at configurable transformer layers (list of layer indices)    - Adds cross-attention output to LLM hidden state via gated residual 4. FiLM-style residual modulation as alternative injection method: scale/shift LLM hidden state based on reservoir state 5. All modules designed for frozen LLM + LoRA: gradient flows through interface params but NOT through reservoir weights 6. Tests in tests/test_interface.py: modules instantiate, forward pass produces correct shapes, gradient flows through interface but stops at reservoir boundary DEPENDS ON: T2 (ESN reservoir, for reservoir state shapes), T3 (model loading, for LLM hidden dims) FILE: src/reservoir/interface.py COMPUTE: CPU only
title: T12: Read/Write interface & cross-attention sidecar module

description: Implement the training pipeline for frozen LLM + LoRA + reservoir interface at src/training/lora_trainer.py. DELIVERABLES: 1. LoRATrainer class with:    - Frozen base LLM + LoRA adapters (via peft library)    - Trainable reservoir interface params (ReadProjection, WriteHead, CrossAttentionSidecar from T12)    - AdamW optimizer for LoRA + interface params only    - Gradient checkpointing for VRAM efficiency    - Mixed precision training (BF16/FP16)    - Wandb logging (loss, lr, grad norms, reservoir stats) 2. Configurable: LoRA rank, target modules (q_proj, v_proj, etc.), lr schedule (cosine with warmup), dataset mixing ratios 3. Checkpoint save/load (model + optimizer + scheduler state) 4. Training data loading from HuggingFace datasets (FineWeb subset + synthetic from T5) 5. Tests in tests/test_trainer.py: pipeline runs a 10-step smoke test without OOM (can mock GPU), loss decreases, checkpoints save/load DEPENDS ON: T3 (model loading for ModelWrapper) FILE: src/training/lora_trainer.py COMPUTE: CPU only (builds pipeline, no actual training). GPU smoke test optional.
title: T13: LoRA + adapter training pipeline

description: Implement the Echo State Network reservoir at src/reservoir/esn.py. DELIVERABLES: 1. ESN class with configurable: size (500-50K nodes), spectral_radius, leak_rate, input_scaling, sparsity 2. Sparse topology generators: Erdos-Renyi and small-world (Watts-Strogatz) 3. State evolution: step(x_t, w_t) -> r_t using leaky-integrator equation r_t = (1-alpha)*r_{t-1} + alpha*tanh(W_res*r_{t-1} + W_in*[x_t; w_t]) 4. Sparse matrix storage (scipy.sparse or torch.sparse) 5. Batched forward for GPU tensors 6. reset() to zero state 7. Unit tests in tests/test_esn.py: echo state property (spectral radius < 1 => state fading), performance (<1ms per step at 50K on CPU), state norm bounded over 10K steps DEPENDS ON: T1 (needs src/ package structure and Reservoir protocol from src/types.py) FILE: src/reservoir/esn.py ACCEPTANCE: All unit tests pass, implements Reservoir protocol from src/types.py.
title: T2: ESN reservoir core library

description: Implement unified model loading at src/models/loader.py. DELIVERABLES: 1. load_model(name, dtype, device) function supporting: Qwen/Qwen3.5-0.8B-Base, meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B, state-spaces/mamba2-1.3b 2. Automatic dtype selection (FP16/BF16 based on GPU capability) 3. Tokenizer wrapper with consistent encode/decode interface across model families 4. Layer-name inspection utility: list_layers(model) returning layer names, types, shapes 5. Specific utility to identify DeltaNet blocks in Qwen3.5 by name 6. ModelWrapper protocol implementation (forward/generate/get_hidden from src/types.py) 7. Unit tests in tests/test_loader.py: model loads (can mock for CI), forward pass produces logits of correct shape, DeltaNet layers identified DEPENDS ON: T1 (needs src/ package structure and ModelWrapper protocol) FILE: src/models/loader.py NOTE: Models will be downloaded on first use. For CI, use mocks. Smoke test on GPU optional.
title: T3: Model loading & tokenizer utilities

description: Implement the evaluation harness at src/eval/harness.py. DELIVERABLES: 1. EvalHarness class that runs any benchmark suite against any ModelWrapper 2. Support for: few-shot prompting (configurable n_shots), greedy and sampling decode, per-task metrics (exact_match, perplexity, accuracy) 3. Batch evaluation for throughput (configurable batch_size) 4. Output: standardized JSON with EvalResult objects (task/metric/value/config) 5. CLI entry point or script to run evaluations from config 6. Unit tests in tests/test_harness.py: harness runs a dummy benchmark against a dummy model, produces valid JSON DEPENDS ON: T1 (needs EvalResult, BenchmarkExample, ModelWrapper, Generator protocols from src/types.py) FILE: src/eval/harness.py ACCEPTANCE: Harness runs with mock model/benchmark, produces valid JSON results file.
title: T4: Evaluation harness

description: Implement benchmark generators at src/eval/benchmarks/. DELIVERABLES: 1. src/eval/benchmarks/memory.py — Memory benchmarks:    - passkey_retrieval(depth, n) — hide a passkey in text, query it    - variable_tracking(n_vars, n_distractors) — track variable assignments through noise    - associative_recall(n_pairs, delay) — store key-value pairs, query after delay 2. src/eval/benchmarks/computation.py — Computation benchmarks:    - multi_digit_arithmetic(n_digits, op) — addition/multiplication with carry    - modular_arithmetic(modulus, n_ops) — chained mod operations    - dyck_language(depth, n_types) — Dyck language recognition    - program_trace(n_steps) — trace variable state through synthetic program execution 3. src/eval/benchmarks/emergent.py — Emergent capability benchmarks:    - compositional_generalization(held_out_ops) — test unseen operator combos    - length_extrapolation(train_len, test_len) — train short, test long    - algorithmic_transfer() — transfer learned algorithms to new domains 4. All generators produce (input, target, metadata) tuples matching BenchmarkExample from src/types.py 5. Configurable difficulty via parameters 6. Tests in tests/test_benchmarks.py: each generator produces 1K+ valid examples, difficulty scales DEPENDS ON: T1 (needs BenchmarkExample dataclass and Generator protocol) FILES: src/eval/benchmarks/memory.py, computation.py, emergent.py
title: T5: Synthetic benchmark suite

description: Implement chaotic systems trajectory generation at src/data/chaos.py. DELIVERABLES: 1. Lorenz-63 integrator: dx/dt = sigma*(y-x), dy/dt = x*(rho-z)-y, dz/dt = x*y - beta*z    Default params: sigma=10, rho=28, beta=8/3. Known Lyapunov exponent lambda ~= 0.906. 2. Mackey-Glass delay-differential: dx/dt = beta*x(t-tau)/(1+x(t-tau)^n) - gamma*x(t)    Variable tau for difficulty control. 3. Kuramoto-Sivashinsky PDE: u_t = -u*u_x - u_xx - u_xxxx    Pseudospectral method, configurable domain size. 4. Common interface: generate(system, n_steps, dt, params) -> trajectory array 5. Train/val/test split utilities (non-overlapping temporal splits) 6. Normalization (per-channel z-score) 7. Lyapunov time computation for evaluation horizon calibration 8. Tests in tests/test_chaos.py: Lorenz Lyapunov exponent matches ~0.906 (within 5%), splits non-overlapping, data loads in <1s DEPENDS ON: T1 (needs DataPipeline protocol from src/types.py) FILE: src/data/chaos.py
title: T6: Chaotic systems data pipeline

description: Evaluate unmodified Qwen3.5-0.8B-Base on full benchmark suite. This is the PRIMARY reference point for all comparisons. DELIVERABLES: 1. Run Qwen3.5-0.8B-Base through the eval harness (T4) on ALL benchmarks:    - Synthetic benchmarks from T5 (memory, computation, emergent)    - Chaotic system prediction from T6    - Standard benchmarks if available (GSM8K, ARC) 2. Record: perplexity on held-out text, accuracy on all synthetic tasks, throughput (tokens/sec), VRAM usage 3. Output: complete results JSON in results/baselines/qwen35_vanilla.json 4. Script: scripts/run_baseline_qwen.py or configs/baselines/qwen35_vanilla.yaml DEPENDS ON: T3 (model loading), T4 (eval harness), T5 (benchmarks), T6 (chaos data) COMPUTE: GPU (~3h) ACCEPTANCE: Complete results JSON for all benchmark categories.
title: T7: Qwen3.5-0.8B-Base vanilla baselines

description: Apply YaRN-style RoPE scaling to Qwen3.5-0.8B and evaluate on long-context benchmarks. This is Control 1 — tests if simple positional scaling solves the memory gap. DELIVERABLES: 1. Implement YaRN-style RoPE scaling for Qwen3.5-0.8B (modify rope_scaling config in transformers) 2. Evaluate on long-context benchmarks: passkey retrieval at 32K/64K/128K+ positions, variable tracking at long range 3. Compare vs T7 vanilla on same benchmarks 4. Output: results JSON in results/baselines/qwen35_yarn.json 5. Script: scripts/run_baseline_yarn.py or configs/baselines/qwen35_yarn.yaml DEPENDS ON: T3 (model loading), T4 (eval harness), T5 (benchmarks) COMPUTE: GPU (~3h) ACCEPTANCE: Long-context eval results at 32K, 64K, 128K+ positions.
title: T8: Qwen3.5-0.8B + RoPE/YaRN context extension baseline

description: Evaluate Mamba-2 1.3B on full benchmark suite. This is Control 2 — tests whether SSMs natively solve the memory gap without reservoir augmentation. DELIVERABLES: 1. Load Mamba-2 1.3B (state-spaces/mamba2-1.3b) via T3 model loader 2. Run through eval harness on ALL benchmarks (T5 synthetic + T6 chaotic) 3. Direct comparison table vs T7 (Qwen3.5 vanilla) 4. Output: results JSON in results/baselines/mamba2_1.3b.json 5. Script: scripts/run_baseline_mamba.py or configs/baselines/mamba2.yaml DEPENDS ON: T3 (model loading), T4 (eval harness), T5 (benchmarks) COMPUTE: GPU (~5h) ACCEPTANCE: Full results JSON, direct comparison table vs T7.
title: T9: Mamba-2 1.3B native SSM baseline

Create the project foundation. Set up pyproject.toml with deps (torch, transformers, reservoirpy, peft, wandb, scipy, ruff, mypy, pytest). Create src/ package layout per IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md Section 7: src/reservoir/, src/models/, src/training/, src/eval/, src/eval/benchmarks/, src/data/. Create tests/ dir. Set up pre-commit with ruff+mypy. Add CI skeleton (.github/workflows/).

Define shared protocols and types in src/types.py (or src/types/):
- ReservoirConfig dataclass (size, spectral_radius, leak_rate, input_scaling, topology)

title: T1: Project scaffolding & shared interfaces

description:
Evaluate LLaMA-3.2-1B (pure softmax attention, no DeltaNet) on the full benchmark suite. Architecture control for the DeltaNet synergy hypothesis — needed to compare against T17 (LLaMA + reservoir).

Script: scripts/eval_llama.py

Evaluation:

- Same metrics as T7 for direct comparison

Output: results/baselines/llama32_1b.json

Depends on: T3 (model loading), T4 (eval harness), T5 (benchmarks).
GPU task, ~3 hours.
title: T10: LLaMA-3.2-1B pure-attention baseline

description:
Implement compressive memory baseline on Qwen3.5-0.8B-Base. This is Control 3 — the strongest non-reservoir memory baseline. Choose one of:
- Infini-attention (compressive memory with linear attention for long-range)
- Titans/ATLAS-style test-time memory module

Implementation in src/models/infini_attention.py or src/models/titans_memory.py:
- Add compressive memory module to selected layers of Qwen3.5
- Train adapter + memory module using same fine-tuning data as Track A will use
- Use LoRA for the base model params

Training:
- Same fine-tuning mix that Track A will use
- LoRA + memory module parameters
- ~5K training steps

Evaluation:
- Full benchmark suite, especially memory benchmarks
- Compare vs T7 vanilla baseline

Output: results/baselines/infini_attention.json (or titans.json)

Acceptance: Trained model exists, eval results on memory benchmarks recorded, comparison vs T7 documented.

Depends on: T3 (model loading), T4 (eval harness), T5 (benchmarks).
GPU task, ~15 hours (implementation + training + eval).
title: T11: Infini-attention / Titans baseline

description:
Implement src/reservoir/interface.py — the bridge between ESN reservoir and LLM hidden states.

Modules to implement:
1. ReadProjection: reservoir state → LLM dimension. Linear projection from reservoir dim to model hidden dim.
2. WriteHead: LLM hidden → reservoir input. Projects LLM hidden states to reservoir input dimension.
3. CrossAttentionSidecar: reservoir states serve as K/V, LLM hidden states serve as Q. Inserted at configurable transformer layers. Multi-head cross-attention with learnable layer norm.
4. FiLM-style residual modulation (alternative injection): γ, β = f(reservoir_state), output = γ * hidden + β. Gated residual connection.

Key design decisions:
- All modules work with FROZEN LLM + LoRA (gradient must NOT flow into reservoir weights)
- Gradient flows through the interface (ReadProjection, WriteHead, CrossAttentionSidecar are trainable)
- Gradient stops at reservoir boundary (reservoir weights are fixed random)
- Configurable: which layers to insert sidecar, attention heads, projection dims

Tests in tests/test_interface.py:
- Modules instantiate with correct shapes
- Forward pass produces correct output shapes
- Gradient flows through interface params but stops at reservoir
- Works with different reservoir sizes and model dims

Depends on: T2 (ESN), T3 (model loading for hidden dim info). CPU only.
title: T12: Read/Write interface & cross-attention sidecar module

description:
Implement src/training/lora_trainer.py — training loop for frozen LLM + LoRA + reservoir interface.

Features:
- LoRATrainer class with train(model, reservoir, interface, dataset, config) method
- Freeze base model, only train: LoRA params + interface params (ReadProjection, WriteHead, CrossAttentionSidecar)
- AdamW optimizer with separate learning rates for LoRA vs interface params
- Gradient checkpointing for VRAM efficiency
- Mixed precision (BF16) training
- Wandb logging (loss, lr, grad norms, reservoir state stats)
- Configurable: LoRA rank (4-64), target modules, learning rate schedule (cosine with warmup), dataset mixing ratios
- Checkpoint save/load (save LoRA + interface weights, not frozen model)
- Early stopping based on validation loss
- Data loading: support for multiple datasets with mixing ratios

Config: configs/lora_training.yaml template

Tests in tests/test_trainer.py:
- Pipeline runs a 10-step smoke test without OOM (can use small dummy model)
- Loss decreases over steps
- Checkpoints save and load correctly
- Only expected params have gradients

Depends on: T3 (model loading). CPU only (builds pipeline, training tested on GPU in smoke test).
title: T13: LoRA + adapter training pipeline

description:
Train Qwen3.5-0.8B-Base with a READ-ONLY reservoir sidecar (no write head). This is the first real LRS experiment.

Architecture:
- Frozen Qwen3.5-0.8B-Base + LoRA adapters
- ESN reservoir processes input embeddings → reservoir states
- ReadProjection + CrossAttentionSidecar inject reservoir memory into LLM at selected layers
- NO WriteHead — reservoir only reads input embeddings, LLM cannot write to reservoir

Training:
- Use T13 LoRA training pipeline
- Fine-tuning mix: ~500M tokens (FineWeb subset + synthetic from T5)
- ~5K training steps
- LoRA rank 16 (starting point), target: attention Q/V projections
- Reservoir config: 10K nodes, spectral radius 0.9, leak rate 0.5 (starting point)

Evaluation:
- Full benchmark suite (T5 + chaotic from T6)
- Compare against T7 (vanilla Qwen3.5) — the key comparison
- Record: perplexity, all task accuracies, throughput, VRAM

Script: scripts/train_track_a_readonly.py
Config: configs/track_a_readonly.yaml
Output: results/track_a/readonly.json

Acceptance: Model trains without divergence, perplexity degradation < 2% vs T7, benchmark results recorded.

Depends on: T5 (benchmarks for eval), T7 (baseline to compare), T12 (sidecar module), T13 (training pipeline).
GPU task, ~30 hours.
title: T14: Track A read-only sidecar training

description:
Add WriteHead to the read-only sidecar (T14) and train the full read/write system.

Architecture:
- Start from T14 checkpoint (read-only pretrained)
- Add WriteHead: LLM hidden states → reservoir input signal
- Gated write: gradually enable write head from 0→1 over first 1K steps (curriculum)

Training:
- Initialize from T14 checkpoint
- Train LoRA + ReadProjection + WriteHead + CrossAttentionSidecar
- Write head gate: linear warmup from 0.0 to 1.0 over first 1K steps
- Same fine-tuning data as T14
- ~5K additional training steps

Evaluation:
- Full benchmark suite
- Compare: read-only (T14) vs read/write (T15) vs vanilla (T7)
- Verify write head is non-trivial: write vector norm > 0, entropy > threshold
- Track reservoir state evolution with vs without write signal

Script: scripts/train_track_a_readwrite.py
Config: configs/track_a_readwrite.yaml
Output: results/track_a/readwrite.json

Acceptance: Write head produces non-trivial signals, read/write model meets or beats read-only on memory benchmarks, perplexity degradation < 2% vs T7.

Depends on: T14 (read-only checkpoint).
GPU task, ~30 hours.
title: T15: Track A full read/write sidecar training

description:
Sweep key reservoir hyperparameters using best config from T15 as starting point. Sequential sweeps (not full grid) to find optimal reservoir configuration.

Sweep dimensions:
1. Reservoir size: 500, 2K, 10K, 50K (4 runs)
2. Spectral radius: 0.5, 0.9, 0.99, 1.1 (4 runs)
3. Leak rate: 0.1, 0.3, 0.7, 1.0 (4 runs)
4. Topology: Erdős–Rényi vs small-world (2 runs)
5. Best combo fine-tune (1 run)

Total: 15 runs. Each run: 1–2K steps with early stopping, eval on memory + computation benchmark subsets (not full suite — speed over completeness).

Method: Sequential sweep — sweep one dimension at a time, fix best value, move to next dimension.

Script: scripts/sweep_reservoir_hp.py
Config: configs/sweep/ directory with per-run configs
Output: results/track_a/sweep/ directory

Acceptance: Pareto frontier identified (reservoir size vs quality vs latency), best config documented, all results logged to wandb.

Depends on: T15 (best read/write checkpoint as starting point).
GPU task, ~80 hours (15 runs × ~5h each). Optionally offload to cloud.
title: T16: Reservoir hyperparameter sweep

description:
Apply the SAME read-only sidecar architecture to LLaMA-3.2-1B (pure softmax attention, no DeltaNet). This tests the DeltaNet synergy hypothesis: does Qwen3.5's DeltaNet architecture provide special compatibility with reservoir states?

Architecture:
- Frozen LLaMA-3.2-1B + LoRA adapters
- Same ESN reservoir config as T14
- Same ReadProjection + CrossAttentionSidecar as T14
- Same training hyperparameters and data as T14

Training:
- Same fine-tuning mix, same number of steps, same LoRA rank
- Only difference: base model is LLaMA-3.2-1B instead of Qwen3.5-0.8B

Evaluation:
- Full benchmark suite — MUST use identical eval as T14 for direct comparison
- Key metric: Δ_Qwen = (T14 - T7) vs Δ_LLaMA = (T17 - T10)
- If Δ_Qwen >> Δ_LLaMA → DeltaNet synergy confirmed
- If Δ_Qwen ≈ Δ_LLaMA → reservoir benefit is architecture-agnostic

Script: scripts/train_llama_reservoir.py
Config: configs/llama_reservoir.yaml
Output: results/track_a/llama_reservoir.json

Acceptance: Results directly comparable to T14, DeltaNet synergy hypothesis has quantitative answer.

Depends on: T10 (LLaMA baseline), T12 (sidecar module), T13 (training pipeline).
GPU task, ~30 hours.
title: T17: LLaMA-3.2-1B + reservoir (DeltaNet synergy control)

description:
Compile ALL Track A results and evaluate against Gate A thresholds. This is a pass/fail decision point.

Gate A Thresholds (ALL must pass for full pass):
1. ≥ 10% gain on long-context retrieval at ≥ 128K context equivalent vs T7
2. ≥ 15% gain on algorithmic memory tasks vs T7
3. ≥ 10% gain on compositional generalization vs T7
4. ≤ 20% inference latency overhead vs T7
5. < 2% perplexity degradation vs T7

Analysis required:
- Comparison table: T7 (vanilla) vs T8 (YaRN) vs T9 (Mamba) vs T10 (LLaMA) vs T11 (Infini-attn) vs T14 (read-only) vs T15 (read/write) vs T16 (best sweep) vs T17 (LLaMA+RC)
- DeltaNet synergy analysis: T17 vs T14 gain comparison
- Statistical significance where applicable
- Reservoir HP sweep summary from T16

Output: docs/gate_a_report.md with all tables, pass/fail per criterion, and recommendation.

Script: scripts/gate_a_analysis.py to generate comparison tables from results/ JSONs.

Acceptance: Written report with pass/fail on each gate criterion, recommendation to proceed or stop, all numbers reproducible from logged artifacts.

Depends on: T7, T8, T9, T10, T11, T15, T16, T17.
CPU (analysis) + brief GPU for any missing evals. ~5 hours.
title: T18: Gate A evaluation & decision report

description:
Implement src/reservoir/ril.py — a module that inserts BETWEEN transformer blocks (deeper integration than T12 sidecar).

Architecture:
- RIL sits between transformer block N and block N+1
- Input: hidden state h from block N output
- Operations:
  1. Read reservoir state r via ReadProjection → memory_signal
  2. Compute write signal from h via WriteHead → w
  3. Update reservoir: reservoir.step(input=current_embedding, write=w)
  4. Modulate hidden state: h' = h + gate * f(memory_signal)
  5. gate is a learnable scalar (initialized to 0 for stable training start)
- Output: modulated h' passed to block N+1

Configurable:
- Insertion points: every k layers (e.g., every 6 blocks in Qwen3.5's 36 layers)
- Gate initialization value
- Whether to share reservoir across RIL instances or use separate reservoirs
- Read/write projection dimensions

Compatible with Qwen3.5 block structure (must handle both DeltaNet and full-attention blocks).

Tests in tests/test_ril.py:
- Module inserts into Qwen3.5 model without shape errors
- Forward pass works end-to-end
- Gradient flows correctly through RIL interface but stops at reservoir
- Gate initialized near zero

Depends on: T2 (ESN), T12 (Read/Write interface). CPU only.
title: T19: Reservoir Interaction Layer (RIL) module

description:
Implement src/reservoir/esn.py — ESN class with configurable size (500–50K nodes), spectral radius, leak rate, input scaling, sparse topology (Erdős–Rényi, small-world). Core method: step(x_t, w_t) → r_t for state evolution. Use sparse matrix storage (scipy.sparse or torch.sparse). Support batched forward for GPU tensors.

Key implementation details:
- Initialize weight matrix W with desired spectral radius (generate random sparse, rescale eigenvalues)
- Input weight matrix W_in: dense or sparse, configurable scaling
- State update: r_t = (1-α)*r_{t-1} + α*tanh(W*r_{t-1} + W_in*x_t + w_t)
- α = leak rate
- Implement both Erdős–Rényi (random edges with probability p) and small-world (Watts-Strogatz) topologies
- reset() method to zero state

Tests in tests/test_esn.py:
- Echo state property: spectral radius < 1 ⟹ state fading memory
- Performance: <1 ms per step at 50K nodes on CPU
- State norm bounded over 10K steps across parameter regimes

Depends on: T1 (for types/protocols). CPU only.
title: T2: ESN reservoir core library

description:
Implement src/reservoir/deltanet_replace.py — replace selected Gated DeltaNet blocks in Qwen3.5 with ESN reservoir modules.

Concept: Qwen3.5-0.8B has ~18 Gated DeltaNet layers interleaved with full-attention layers. This module replaces selected DeltaNet blocks with ESN reservoir modules that serve as 'richer' recurrent modules with higher-dimensional state.

Implementation:
- DeltaNetReplacer class that takes a Qwen3.5 model and a list of DeltaNet layer indices to replace
- For each replaced layer: remove DeltaNet attention, insert ESN reservoir with read/write interface
- Surrounding full-attention layers and FFN layers remain unchanged
- The replacement module must match input/output dimensions of the original DeltaNet block
- Configurable: which DeltaNet blocks to replace (some or all of the 18 DeltaNet layers)
- Support A/B swap: ability to dynamically switch between DeltaNet and ESN for controlled experiments

Uses T3's layer-name inspection to identify DeltaNet layers by name.
Uses T12's ReadProjection/WriteHead for the reservoir interface.

Tests in tests/test_deltanet_replace.py:
- Model with replaced blocks produces valid output of correct shape
- Parameter count accounting is correct (report params added/removed)
- DeltaNet vs ESN A/B swap works without errors
- Can replace 1, 6, or all 18 DeltaNet layers

Depends on: T3 (layer identification), T12 (interface). CPU only.
title: T20: DeltaNet block replacement module

description:
Implement src/reservoir/multi_reservoir.py — parallel reservoir configuration with different timescales.

Architecture:
- Fast reservoir: high leak rate (α ≈ 0.7–1.0), spectral radius near 1.0, for computational scratch-space. Responds quickly to recent input, short memory horizon.
- Slow reservoir: low leak rate (α ≈ 0.1–0.3), spectral radius near 0.5, for long-term contextual memory. Smooths over long sequences, retains information longer.

Interface:
- Both reservoirs receive the same input (or separate inputs via separate write heads)
- Shared read interface: LLM cross-attends to CONCATENATED reservoir states [r_fast; r_slow]
- Independent write heads per reservoir (LLM can write different signals to fast vs slow)
- MultiReservoir class wrapping two ESN instances with coordinated step/reset

Configurable:
- Fast/slow reservoir sizes (can be different)
- Independent spectral radius, leak rate, topology per reservoir
- Whether write heads are shared or independent
- Concatenation vs attention-based state merging

Tests in tests/test_multi_reservoir.py:
- Dual-reservoir state evolves at different timescales (verified by autocorrelation of state trajectories — fast reservoir autocorrelation decays faster)
- Read interface produces correct shapes (concatenated dim = fast_dim + slow_dim)
- Step and reset work correctly for both reservoirs
- Each reservoir maintains independent state

Depends on: T2 (ESN core). CPU only.
title: T21: Multi-reservoir (fast/slow) module

description:
Train three Track B configurations on Qwen3.5-0.8B-Base. Track B uses DEEPER integration than Track A's bolt-on sidecar.

Three configurations to train and evaluate:

1. RIL insertion (T19): Reservoir interaction layers every 6 blocks in Qwen3.5
   - LoRA + RIL interface params
   - Selective unfreezing of adjacent LayerNorms

2. DeltaNet replacement (T20): Replace 6 of 18 DeltaNet blocks with ESN
   - LoRA + replacement interface params
   - Start with replacing every 3rd DeltaNet layer

3. Multi-reservoir RIL (T21 + T19): Fast/slow dual reservoirs with RIL insertion
   - LoRA + dual-reservoir RIL interface params
   - Same insertion schedule as config 1

Training for all configs:
- Same fine-tuning data as Track A
- Same LoRA setup (rank 16, Q/V targets)
- ~5-10K steps each
- Wandb logging per config
- Best reservoir HP from T16 sweep

Evaluation:
- Full benchmark suite for each config
- Direct comparison: Config 1 vs 2 vs 3 vs Track A best vs T7 baseline

Scripts: scripts/train_track_b_{ril,deltanet,multi}.py
Configs: configs/track_b/
Output: results/track_b/

Acceptance: All three configs train stably, results comparable across configs, best config identified.

Depends on: T18 (Gate A must pass), T19, T20, T21.
GPU task, ~120 hours (3 configs × 40h each).
title: T22: Track B training & evaluation

description:
Evaluate Track B against Gate B thresholds. Pass/fail decision for proceeding to Track C.

Gate B Thresholds:
1. ≥ 20% exact-match gain on long program-trace tasks vs T7
2. Better memory-quality-per-byte than RoPE/YaRN (T8) — i.e., achieve same or better memory quality with fewer additional parameters/memory
3. < 2% perplexity degradation vs T7

Analysis:
- Comparison: Track B best vs Track A best vs all baselines
- Which insertion strategy works best (RIL vs DeltaNet replacement vs multi-reservoir)
- Does multi-reservoir outperform single reservoir?
- Efficiency analysis: gains per additional parameter, per additional FLOP
- Architecture recommendation for Track C

Output: docs/gate_b_report.md

Script: scripts/gate_b_analysis.py

Acceptance: Written report with gate pass/fail, Track A vs B comparison, architectural recommendation for Track C.

Depends on: T22.
CPU + GPU, ~10 hours.
title: T23: Gate B evaluation & decision report

description:
Define the from-scratch hybrid architecture in src/models/rw_transformer.py — the Reservoir-Workspace Transformer.

Architecture (decoder-only, ~0.8B trainable parameters):
- Each decoder block has THREE parallel branches:
  1. Local/global attention branch (standard causal attention)
  2. MLP branch (standard FFN)
  3. Bidirectional multi-reservoir workspace (fast + slow ESN from T21)
- Gated residual mixing layer to fuse the three branches: h' = h + g1*attn_out + g2*mlp_out + g3*reservoir_out
- g1, g2, g3 are learnable gate scalars (initialized so reservoir gate starts near 0)

Design constraints:
- ~0.8B trainable parameters (transformer + interfaces), reservoir weights frozen
- Same tokenizer as Qwen3.5 (248K vocab) for fair comparison
- Compatible with gradient checkpointing for A100 training
- Compatible with mixed precision (BF16)

Configuration:
- Number of layers, hidden dim, attention heads — sized to hit ~0.8B params
- Reservoir insertion: every N layers or at specific layer indices
- Fast/slow reservoir sizes (from T21)

Output: src/models/rw_transformer.py with RWTransformer class

Tests in tests/test_rw_transformer.py:
- Model instantiates at ~0.8B params (verify parameter count)
- Forward pass produces logits of correct shape
- Backward pass works (gradients flow correctly)
- Architecture diagram documented in docstring or separate doc

Depends on: T2 (ESN core), T21 (multi-reservoir). CPU only.
title: T24: RW-Transformer architecture definition

description:
Implement src/training/curriculum.py — the three-stage training curriculum for Track C pre-training.

Stage 1: Standard next-token prediction
- General text from FineWeb/RedPajama
- Standard causal LM objective
- Context length: 4K tokens

Stage 2: Procedural objective mixing
- Mix general text with procedural tasks:
  - Arithmetic traces (from T5 computation benchmarks)
  - Symbolic state tracking
  - Dyck language sequences
- Dynamic mixing ratios (configurable, e.g., 70% text / 30% procedural)
- Context length: 4K tokens

Stage 3: Length curriculum
- Gradually increase context length: 4K → 8K → 16K → 32K → 128K
- Maintain procedural mix from Stage 2
- Optional: auxiliary reservoir-state prediction loss (predict next reservoir state as regularizer)

Implementation:
- CurriculumDataPipeline class implementing DataPipeline protocol
- Streaming data loading (datasets library with streaming=True for large corpus)
- Dynamic mixing ratios per stage
- Automatic stage transitions based on step count or loss plateau
- Support for resuming from checkpoint at any stage

Tests in tests/test_curriculum.py:
- Data pipeline produces batches at each curriculum stage
- Mixing ratios are correct
- Streaming works (doesn't load full corpus into memory)
- Stage transitions work correctly

Depends on: T5 (benchmark generators for procedural data). CPU only.
title: T25: Training curriculum & data pipeline

description:
Pre-train the RW-Transformer (T24) from scratch using the curriculum (T25) on cloud A100(s).

Training setup:
- Model: RW-Transformer from T24 (~0.8B trainable params)
- Data: Curriculum from T25 (Stages 1→2→3)
- Optimizer: AdamW (full, not LoRA — training from scratch)
- Precision: BF16 mixed precision
- Gradient checkpointing: enabled (required for A100 80GB)
- Target: ~20B tokens minimum (may extend if loss is still decreasing)
- LR schedule: cosine with warmup (warmup ~2K steps)

Cloud infrastructure:
- Provider: RunPod or Lambda (A100 80GB spot instances)
- Estimated: ~500 hours A100
- Cost: $250-400 at spot pricing
- Setup scripts for cloud environment

Monitoring:
- Wandb logging: loss, perplexity, learning rate, gradient norms, reservoir state statistics
- Periodic eval checkpoints (every ~2B tokens) on T5 benchmark subset
- Early stopping if loss diverges

Scripts: scripts/train_track_c.py, scripts/cloud_setup.sh
Config: configs/track_c_pretrain.yaml

Acceptance: Training loss converges, perplexity within +3% of Qwen3.5-0.8B baseline (T7) on held-out text.

Depends on: T23 (Gate B must pass), T24 (RW-Transformer), T25 (curriculum).
CLOUD GPU (A100 80GB), ~500 hours.
title: T26: Track C pre-training

description:
Test multi-sub-step reservoir evolution between tokens — the core 'latent reasoning' hypothesis.

Concept: Between generating token t and token t+1, let the reservoir evolve for K additional sub-steps without new input. This gives the reservoir 'thinking time' — latent computation in reservoir state space.

Experiments:

1. K sweep: K = 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 sub-steps between token generations
   - For each K: evaluate on full benchmark suite
   - Plot: accuracy vs K for each task category

2. Halting strategies:
   a. Fixed K (baseline)
   b. Convergence-based: stop when ‖r^(k) − r^(k−1)‖ < ε
   c. Learned halting: PonderNet-style (learn a halting probability per step)

3. Coconut comparison (CRITICAL):
   - Implement Coconut-style hidden-state recirculation on Qwen3.5-0.8B
   - Same compute budget as reservoir latent reasoning
   - Direct comparison: Coconut vs reservoir latent thinking at matched FLOPs

4. Eval on reasoning benchmarks:
   - ProsQA (multi-hop deduction)
   - Multi-hop deduction tasks
   - Track C gate benchmarks

Scripts: scripts/latent_reasoning_sweep.py, scripts/coconut_baseline.py
Config: configs/latent_reasoning/
Output: results/track_c/latent_reasoning/

Acceptance: K scaling curve documented, comparison vs Coconut at matched compute budget, best halting strategy identified.

Depends on: T26 (pre-trained RW-Transformer).
GPU (local for small K) + Cloud (large K sweep), ~40h local + ~50h cloud.
title: T27: Latent reasoning experiments (K sub-steps)

description:
Evaluate Track C against Gate C thresholds. Final pass/fail gate.

Gate C Thresholds:
1. Match baseline perplexity within +3% (vs T7 Qwen3.5 vanilla)
2. ≥ 25% gain on long-horizon memory tasks vs T7
3. O(1) memory scaling for workspace state — better inference memory efficiency than dense attention at long horizons

Analysis:
- Full comparison table: Track A best vs Track B best vs Track C vs ALL baselines (T7-T11)
- Scaling analysis: how does quality scale with context length? With K sub-steps?
- Memory efficiency: VRAM usage at 32K, 64K, 128K contexts for reservoir vs dense attention
- Latent reasoning analysis: which halting strategy works best? Does K>1 help?
- Coconut comparison summary

Output: docs/gate_c_report.md

Script: scripts/gate_c_analysis.py

Acceptance: Comprehensive comparison table across all tracks and baselines, gate pass/fail, scaling analysis documented.

Depends on: T27.
CPU + GPU, ~10 hours.
title: T28: Gate C evaluation & decision report

description:
Run ablation studies on the best-performing track to understand WHAT is driving the gains (or lack thereof).

Ablation experiments (on best track's model):
1. Read-only vs write-only vs full read/write
2. Single reservoir vs multi-reservoir (fast/slow)
3. Frozen vs partially trainable reservoir input projections
4. Spectral radius regimes: subcritical (0.5) vs critical (0.99) vs supercritical (1.1)
5. CRITICAL — Randomized dynamics control: replace ESN with random stateless projection (same param count, but no recurrent dynamics). If this performs similarly → gains come from extra parameters, not recurrence. If it performs worse → recurrent dynamics matter.

Efficiency benchmarking:
- Throughput: tokens/sec at batch sizes 1, 4, 16
- Latency: p50/p95 at different sequence lengths
- VRAM: at 4K, 32K, 128K contexts
- Quality-per-FLOP analysis
- Quality-per-byte (parameter efficiency)
- Compare against all baselines at matched compute

Coconut comparison (if not already done thoroughly in T27):
- Coconut vs reservoir latent reasoning at matched compute budget

Scripts: scripts/ablations.py, scripts/efficiency_benchmark.py
Output: results/ablations/, results/efficiency/

Acceptance: All ablation results in standardized JSON, efficiency numbers documented, randomized control confirms (or refutes) that recurrent dynamics matter.

Depends on: T18 (minimum — runs after best completed gate).
GPU task, ~60 hours.
title: T29: Ablation studies & efficiency benchmarking

description:
Implement src/models/loader.py — unified interface to load models in FP16/BF16:
- Qwen3.5-0.8B-Base (hybrid DeltaNet + full-attention architecture)
- LLaMA-3.2-1B (pure softmax attention)
- Mamba-2 1.3B (SSM architecture)

Features:
- load_model(model_name, dtype, device) → ModelWrapper
- Tokenizer wrappers with consistent API across model families
- Layer-name inspection utilities: list all layer names, identify DeltaNet vs full-attention layers in Qwen3.5, identify LoRA target modules
- get_hidden_states(model, input_ids, layer_indices) utility
- VRAM estimation before loading

The ModelWrapper should implement the protocol from src/types.py (forward/generate/get_hidden).

Acceptance: All three model families load on GPU, forward pass produces logits of correct shape, Qwen3.5 DeltaNet layers identified by name. Add tests in tests/test_loader.py (can be smoke tests that mock model loading for CI).

Depends on: T1 (for ModelWrapper protocol). CPU only (downloads models, no training). Brief GPU smoke test.
title: T3: Model loading & tokenizer utilities

description:
Write up results and prepare for release. This includes negative-result reporting if any gate failed (per proposal commitment).

Paper sections:
1. Introduction & motivation (latent reservoir scratchpads concept)
2. Related work (reservoir computing, memory augmentation, Coconut, Infini-attention, Titans)
3. Method (ESN sidecar, RIL insertion, DeltaNet replacement, multi-reservoir, RW-Transformer)
4. Experimental setup (models, benchmarks, training details)
5. Results per track (Track A/B/C, with gate reports)
6. Ablation analysis (from T29)
7. Discussion (DeltaNet synergy, latent reasoning, what worked/didn't)
8. Conclusion

Figures to prepare:
- Architecture diagrams (sidecar, RIL, RW-Transformer)
- Benchmark comparison tables and bar charts
- Scaling curves (quality vs context length, quality vs K sub-steps)
- Pareto frontier (quality vs efficiency)
- Ablation results visualization

Code release:
- Clean up src/ code, add docstrings to public APIs
- Write README.md with installation, quickstart, reproduction instructions
- Ensure all configs in configs/ are documented
- Benchmark harness independently runnable
- Add LICENSE file

Output: docs/paper/ directory with LaTeX source, figures/ directory

Acceptance: Draft paper with all figures, code repo documented and releasable, benchmark harness independently runnable.

Depends on: T29. CPU only.
title: T30: Paper draft & open-source release

description:
Implement src/eval/harness.py — run any benchmark suite against any model, output standardized JSON.

Features:
- evaluate(model: ModelWrapper, benchmarks: list[Generator], config: EvalConfig) → list[EvalResult]
- Support few-shot prompting (0-shot, k-shot with examples)
- Greedy and sampling decode modes
- Per-task metrics: exact match, perplexity, accuracy, F1
- Batch evaluation for throughput (configurable batch size)
- JSON output format with full reproducibility info (model name, config, timestamp, git hash)
- Progress tracking (tqdm or similar)
- Resume from partial results (append mode)

The harness should be model-agnostic — works with any ModelWrapper. Benchmarks provide (input, target, metadata) via the Generator protocol from src/types.py.

Tests in tests/test_harness.py:
- Harness runs a dummy benchmark against a dummy model
- Produces valid JSON results matching EvalResult schema
- Metrics computed correctly on known inputs

Depends on: T1 (for protocols). CPU only.
title: T4: Evaluation harness

description:
Implement benchmark generators in src/eval/benchmarks/:

src/eval/benchmarks/memory.py:
- Passkey retrieval: hide a passkey at configurable depth in distractor text, query at end. Configurable: context length, passkey position, distractor type.
- Variable tracking: define variables, perform assignments across distractors, query final value. Configurable: num variables, num operations, distractor length.
- Associative recall: present key-value pairs, add distractor, query by key. Configurable: num pairs, delay length.

src/eval/benchmarks/computation.py:
- Multi-digit addition/multiplication with carry (configurable digit count)
- Modular arithmetic (a op b mod m, configurable operand size and modulus)
- Dyck language recognition (balanced parentheses, configurable depth and length)
- Synthetic program execution traces (simple variable assignments and arithmetic)

src/eval/benchmarks/emergent.py:
- Compositional generalization: train on subset of operators, test on held-out combinations
- Length extrapolation: generate at train length, test at 2x-10x length
- Algorithmic transfer: train on one algorithm, test on related algorithm

All generators produce BenchmarkExample(input, target, metadata) tuples with configurable difficulty. Each generator must produce 1K+ valid examples. Format must be compatible with T4 harness (Generator protocol from src/types.py).

Tests in tests/test_benchmarks.py. Depends on: T1. CPU only.
title: T5: Synthetic benchmark suite

description:
Implement src/data/chaos.py — generate trajectories for chaotic dynamical systems.

Systems to implement:
1. Lorenz-63: dx/dt = σ(y-x), dy/dt = x(ρ-z)-y, dz/dt = xy-βz. Standard params: σ=10, ρ=28, β=8/3.
2. Mackey-Glass: dx/dt = β*x(t-τ)/(1+x(t-τ)^n) - γ*x(t). Variable τ for different chaos levels.
3. Kuramoto-Sivashinsky: u_t = -u*u_x - u_xx - u_xxxx. PDE, discretize spatially.

Features:
- generate_trajectory(system, params, T, dt, transient) → array
- Train/val/test splits (temporal, non-overlapping)
- Normalization (per-variable z-score)
- Lyapunov time computation for evaluation horizon calibration
- DataLoader-compatible output

Validation:
- Lorenz-63 max Lyapunov exponent λ ≈ 0.906 (verify numerically)
- Mackey-Glass transitions to chaos at τ ≈ 17
- KS spatial statistics match known results

Tests in tests/test_chaos.py:
- Generated trajectories match known Lyapunov exponents
- Splits are non-overlapping
- Data loads in <1s

Depends on: T1. CPU only.
title: T6: Chaotic systems data pipeline

description:
Evaluate unmodified Qwen3.5-0.8B-Base on the full benchmark suite (T5 benchmarks + T6 chaotic systems). This is the PRIMARY reference point for all subsequent comparisons.

Script: scripts/run_baselines.py or scripts/eval_qwen_vanilla.py

Record:
- Perplexity on held-out text
- Accuracy on all synthetic tasks (memory, computation, emergent benchmarks from T5)
- Chaotic prediction quality (trajectory prediction vs Lyapunov time from T6)
- Throughput (tokens/sec)
- VRAM usage
- Inference latency (p50, p95)

Output: results/baselines/qwen35_vanilla.json (standardized EvalResult format from T4 harness)

Acceptance: Complete results JSON for ALL benchmark categories, baseline numbers recorded and reproducible.

Depends on: T3 (model loading), T4 (eval harness), T5 (benchmarks), T6 (chaos data).
GPU task, ~3 hours.
title: T7: Qwen3.5-0.8B-Base vanilla baselines

description:
Apply YaRN-style RoPE scaling to extend Qwen3.5-0.8B-Base context window. This is Control 1 from the proposal — tests whether simple positional extension solves the memory gap.

Implementation:
- Apply YaRN (Yet another RoPE extensioN) scaling to RoPE embeddings
- Use transformers library's rope_scaling config if available, or implement manually
- Scale factor to extend from native context to 128K+

Evaluation:
- Long-context benchmarks: passkey retrieval at 32K, 64K, 128K+ positions
- Variable tracking at long range
- Compare directly against T7 vanilla baselines

Output: results/baselines/qwen35_yarn.json

Acceptance: Long-context eval results at 32K, 64K, 128K+ positions recorded. Direct comparison table vs T7.

Depends on: T3 (model loading), T4 (eval harness), T5 (benchmarks).
GPU task, ~3 hours.
title: T8: Qwen3.5-0.8B + RoPE/YaRN context extension

description:
Evaluate Mamba-2 (1.3B) on the full benchmark suite. This is Control 2 — tests whether SSM architectures natively solve the memory gap without reservoir augmentation.

Script: scripts/eval_mamba2.py

Evaluation:
- Full benchmark suite from T5 (memory, computation, emergent)
- Same metrics as T7 for direct comparison
- Throughput and VRAM comparison

Output: results/baselines/mamba2_1.3b.json

Acceptance: Full results JSON, direct comparison table vs T7 (Qwen3.5 vanilla).

Depends on: T3 (model loading — Mamba-2 support), T4 (eval harness), T5 (benchmarks).
GPU task, ~5 hours.
title: T9: Mamba-2 1.3B native SSM baseline
